Markit Example

This is an example Markit document, which illustrates all the features of the language.

Markit Document Structure

A Markit document is made up of a series of blocks, which are separated by one or more blank lines.

There are three types of blocks: ID blocks, metadata blocks, and content blocks.

ID blocks consist of one or more hash symbols followed by an identifier. They are used to give a text (or any of its child texts) a unique ID, and encode the hierarchical structure of the document. The number of hash symbols indicates the level of the block in the hierarchy. Every document must have a single top-level ID block. Other level documents can be nested inside, to arbitrary depth.

ID blocks may (but need not) be followed by a metadata block. Metadata blocks consist of one or more lines of the form `key: value` (see the metadata block at the top of this document for examples). Metadata blocks are used to attach arbitrary metadata to the text (or any of its child texts).

Content blocks must begin with a block tag, e.g. `{#1}`. A block tag gives a unique ID to the block (relative to the text it is in). Typically IDs are consecutive numbers, but they can be any alphanumeric string.

Block tags can optionally include block metadata, as a comma-separated list of `key=value` pairs (see the example block tag above).

After the block tag, the content of the block follows. Various markup and formatting can be used in the content, as described in the next section.

Markit Markup

Headings

The 0th Block is the Title

Use Pounds on Either Side to Create Headings

The Number Indicates the Heading Level

The Heading Level Can Go Up to 6

You can include regular (non-heading) content in the same block as the heading.

You can also include headings in subsequent blocks

if you want

Formatting

This is a paragraph with some inline markup, and also some nested formatting. Asterisks indicate bold text, and underscores indicate italic text.

Bold and italic text can be used in headings

You can indicate that some text is an inline quotation by surrounding it in double quotation marks: "like this".

You can indicate that some text is a block quotation by surrounding it in two double quotation marks:

    This is a block quotation

You can optionally add single line breaks and indentation around block quotation marks (as shown here), for readability. But don't include multiple line breaks, because a blank line will indicate the start of a new content block.

You can mark up text as a citation by enclosing it in square brackets: [like this].

You can indicate editorial changes by surrounding  and inserted text in double plus signs.

You can indicate that some text is a margin comment by surrounding it in "at" symbols: .

Whitespace

Whitespace inside content is generally ignored by Markit - line breaks are treated as spaces, and multiple consecutive spaces are treated as a single space.

If you want to include line breaks in the content, you can use two forward slashes:
This will ensure that the line break is preserved in the output.

If you want to include multiple consecutive spaces, you can use the tilde character. A single " " represents a single space, but multiple consecutive tildes will be preserved as multiple spaces in the output. Two consecutive tildes "  " are equivalent to a "tab" space.

Foreign Text

Source texts often include foreign text, and it is often useful to be able to indicate that a particular piece of text is in a different language. Foreign text should be enclosed in dollar symbols: like this.

Text enclosed in pairs of dollar symbols - λικε θις - is treated as Greek text. In Greek text, Roman characters or character-pairs are replaced with their Greek equivalents, e.g. φιλοσοφια becomes "φιλοσοφία".

Brace Codes

Some characters commonly found in source texts are difficult to input using standard keyboards. Markit provides a convenient way to input these characters, using "brace codes".

A brace code consists of a sequence of characters enclosed in curly braces: {like this}. The following brace codes are supported: æ (converted to "æ"), œ (converted to "œ"), Æ (converted to "Æ"), Œ (converted to "Œ"), § (converted to "§"), – (converted to "–"), and — (converted to "—").

Page Breaks

Page breaks in the copytext can be indicated using the pipe character "|". In plain text output, page breaks are removed. In HTML output, page breaks are rendered as `<span class="page-break"></span>`, and you can use CSS styling to determine whether or how to render them.

Footnotes

Footnotes should be represented as content blocks line any other, except that their ID should being with an "n", and they should all be placed at the end of the text.

Footnotes are referred to in the main text using a footnote marker, which consists of the footnote ID enclosed in angle brackets: <n1>.

[^n1]: This is a footnote.

Escaping

If you want to include any of the special characters used in Markit markup (e.g. `*`, `_`, `"`, `[`, `]`, etc.) as literal characters in the content, you can escape them using a backslash, e.g. `\*`, `\_`, `\"`, `\[`, `\]`, etc.

Note that the source Markit code here itself has to use backslashes in front of all the special characters above, as well as in front of the backslashes themselves.
